name: History
description: Journey through ancient civilizations, empires, wars, and iconic leaders.

questions.text: In which year did World War II end?
questions.explanation: World War II ended in 1945 with Germany's surrender in May and Japan's surrender in September.

questions.text: Who was the first President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George Washington became the first President of the United States in 1789.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Lighthouse of Alexandria (Pharos) was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, located on the island of Pharos.

questions.text: What year did the Berlin Wall fall?
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, marking a pivotal moment in the end of the Cold War.

questions.text: Who was the Egyptian queen who allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony?
questions.explanation: Cleopatra VII formed political and romantic alliances with Julius Caesar and later Mark Antony.

questions.text: In which country did the Industrial Revolution begin?
questions.explanation: The Industrial Revolution began in Great Britain in the mid-18th century, driven by innovations in textiles and steam power.

questions.text: What famous wall was built to protect China's northern borders?
questions.explanation: The Great Wall of China was built over centuries, primarily during the Ming Dynasty, to protect against northern invasions.

questions.text: Who wrote the Declaration of Independence?
questions.explanation: Thomas Jefferson was the principal author of the Declaration of Independence, adopted on July 4, 1776.

questions.text: Which empire was ruled by Genghis Khan?
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan founded and ruled the Mongol Empire, which became the largest contiguous land empire in history.

questions.text: What was the name of the ship that sank on its maiden voyage in 1912?
questions.explanation: The RMS Titanic sank on April 15, 1912, after striking an iceberg on its first voyage from Southampton to New York.

questions.text: Which civilization built the Machu Picchu complex in Peru?
questions.explanation: Machu Picchu was built by the Inca civilization around 1450 CE and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.text: In which year did the French Revolution begin?
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789, marked by the storming of the Bastille on July 14.

questions.text: Who was the leader of Nazi Germany during World War II?
questions.explanation: Adolf Hitler served as Führer of Nazi Germany from 1934 until his death in 1945.

questions.text: Which Roman emperor converted to Christianity and issued the Edict of Milan?
questions.explanation: Constantine I issued the Edict of Milan in 313 CE, granting religious tolerance and effectively legalizing Christianity.

questions.text: What year did Columbus first reach the Americas?
questions.explanation: Christopher Columbus first arrived in the Americas on October 12, 1492, landing in the Bahamas.

questions.text: Which war was fought between the North and South in the United States?
questions.explanation: The American Civil War (1861–1865) was fought between the Union (North) and the Confederacy (South) over slavery and states' rights.

questions.text: Who was the first man to walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.

questions.text: What was the name of the economic recovery program the US offered to Europe after WWII?
questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided over $13 billion in aid to help rebuild Western European economies after WWII.

questions.text: In which battle was Napoleon Bonaparte finally defeated in 1815?
questions.explanation: Napoleon was decisively defeated at the Battle of Waterloo on June 18, 1815, ending his rule.

questions.text: Which treaty ended World War I?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles, signed in June 1919, formally ended World War I and imposed harsh penalties on Germany.

questions.text: What was the main cause of the 1347–1351 Black Death pandemic?
questions.explanation: The Black Death was caused by Yersinia pestis, a bacterium spread primarily by fleas on rats.

questions.text: Which country was the first to grant women the right to vote nationally?
questions.explanation: New Zealand became the first self-governing country to grant women the right to vote in 1893.

questions.text: Who was the last Tsar of Russia before the 1917 Revolution?
questions.explanation: Nicholas II was the last Russian Tsar, who abdicated in 1917 and was executed with his family in 1918.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in which year?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was sealed by King John of England at Runnymede on June 15, 1215.

questions.text: Which civilization invented cuneiform writing?
questions.explanation: The Sumerians of ancient Mesopotamia developed cuneiform, one of the earliest writing systems, around 3100 BCE.

questions.text: In which modern country is the ancient city of Carthage located?
questions.explanation: Ancient Carthage was located near modern-day Tunis, the capital of present-day Tunisia.

questions.text: Which empire was ruled by Suleiman the Magnificent?
questions.explanation: Suleiman the Magnificent ruled the Ottoman Empire from 1520 to 1566, its most expansive and powerful period.

questions.text: What event triggered the start of World War I?
questions.explanation: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, triggered the chain of events leading to WWI.

questions.text: Who led India's independence movement through nonviolent civil disobedience?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi led India's independence movement using nonviolent civil disobedience, inspiring independence in 1947.

questions.text: Which pharaoh is most associated with the construction of the Great Pyramid at Giza?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu (Cheops) around 2560 BCE.

questions.text: The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648) was primarily fought in which region?
questions.explanation: The Thirty Years' War was fought mostly in the territories of the Holy Roman Empire (modern Germany), devastating much of Central Europe.

questions.text: Which ancient Greek philosopher was sentenced to death and forced to drink hemlock?
questions.explanation: Socrates was tried for impiety and corrupting the youth, found guilty, and executed by drinking hemlock in 399 BCE.

questions.text: What was the name of the secret police of the Soviet Union under Stalin?
questions.explanation: The NKVD (People's Commissariat for Internal Affairs) was the Soviet secret police during Stalin's purges in the 1930s–40s.

questions.text: Which battle in 732 CE stopped the Muslim advance into Western Europe?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Tours (or Poitiers) in 732 CE, won by Charles Martel, halted the Umayyad Caliphate's expansion into Western Europe.

questions.text: Who was the first Emperor of a unified China?
questions.explanation: Qin Shi Huang unified the warring Chinese states in 221 BCE and became the first emperor of a unified China.

questions.text: What was the significance of the Sykes-Picot Agreement (1916)?
options: It ended World War I between Allied powers
It secretly divided the Ottoman Middle East between Britain and France
It established the League of Nations
It created the state of Israel
questions.explanation: The Sykes-Picot Agreement was a secret deal between Britain and France to divide the Arab provinces of the Ottoman Empire into spheres of influence.

questions.text: Which emperor issued the Edict of Fontainebleau in 1685, revoking religious rights of French Protestants?
questions.explanation: Louis XIV issued the Edict of Fontainebleau (Revocation of the Edict of Nantes) in 1685, stripping French Protestants (Huguenots) of religious freedoms.

questions.text: The Meiji Restoration in Japan began in which year?
questions.explanation: The Meiji Restoration began in 1868 when Emperor Meiji was restored to power, launching Japan's rapid modernization and industrialization.

questions.text: Which Roman general crossed the Rubicon River in 49 BCE, triggering a civil war?
questions.explanation: Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon in 49 BCE with his army—an illegal act—igniting a civil war against Pompey and the Senate.

questions.text: What was the Scramble for Africa?
options: A series of African civil wars in the 20th century
The rapid colonization of Africa by European powers in the late 19th century
The migration of Africans to European colonies
A trade competition between African kingdoms
questions.explanation: The Scramble for Africa (1881–1914) was the rapid invasion and partition of Africa by European powers, leaving most of the continent colonized.

questions.text: Who led the Haitian Revolution that resulted in Haiti becoming independent in 1804?
questions.explanation: Toussaint Louverture led the Haitian Revolution, though he died before independence; Jean-Jacques Dessalines declared independence in 1804.

questions.text: What was the primary purpose of the Congress of Vienna (1815)?
options: To punish France for the Napoleonic Wars
To reorganize Europe after Napoleon's defeat and prevent future revolutions
To end the Thirty Years' War
questions.explanation: The Congress of Vienna reorganized Europe to restore balance of power and conservative order after the upheaval of the Napoleonic Wars.

questions.text: Which ancient trade network connected China to the Mediterranean world?
questions.explanation: The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes linking East Asia, Central Asia, and the Mediterranean, facilitating commerce and cultural exchange.

questions.text: The Peloponnesian War was fought between which two Greek city-states?
questions.explanation: The Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) was fought between Athens and Sparta along with their respective allies, ending in Athenian defeat.

questions.text: What was the policy of apartheid in South Africa?
options: A system of racial segregation and white minority rule
An economic embargo against foreign nations
A land reform policy redistributing farms
A military alliance with neighboring states
questions.explanation: Apartheid (1948–1994) was South Africa's institutionalized system of racial segregation that systematically oppressed non-white citizens.

questions.text: Which dynasty built the Forbidden City in Beijing?
questions.explanation: The Forbidden City was constructed during the early Ming Dynasty (1406–1420) and served as the imperial palace for 24 emperors.

questions.text: Who was the Byzantine Emperor during the fall of Constantinople in 1453?
questions.explanation: Constantine XI Palaiologos was the last Byzantine Emperor, dying during the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453.

questions.text: What was the Long March (1934–1935) in Chinese history?
options: A Japanese military campaign across China
A protest march by Chinese workers in Shanghai
A strategic retreat by Mao's Red Army covering over 9,000 km
A trade expedition along the Silk Road
questions.explanation: The Long March was a year-long retreat by Mao Zedong's Communist Red Army, covering ~9,000 km to escape Nationalist forces.

questions.text: Which empire's collapse in 1918 gave rise to modern Turkey, along with several Middle Eastern and Balkan states?
questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire's defeat in WWI led to its dissolution, from which emerged modern Turkey and mandates that became today's Middle Eastern states.

questions.text: The Balfour Declaration of 1917 expressed British support for what?
options: Establishment of a Jewish homeland in Palestine
Arab sovereignty over the Arabian Peninsula
Creation of a League of Nations
questions.explanation: The Balfour Declaration was a 1917 letter from British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour expressing support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.
